comment.body:
Hey dude, obviously I have read it. In the the https://stochvoltrader.substack.com/p/market-analysis
post where she repeatedly referenced, it says surprises hits mid summer - which by definition is June 24. In that post she mentioned watching May 4-6 for key events. 

So now in the latest post, Alma adjusted her bias (which is perfectly fine) - now we're looking at July-August for tariff shock. All I'm asking is if May 4-6 is still a key factor to consider. 

Look if you're that smart and fully comprehended everything - you can either shut up or ignore the post. You reply is not helping at all

comment.body:
the main theme under a bear market seems to be:
1. very little real investor interests and the entire market is rigged by large institutions to distribute shares to de-risk to those who are willing to be harvested in the end
2. any bull rally is a based on the above rigged market manipulation so it can die unexpectedly with no logical explanation after the institutions have distributed their shares (when institutions leave the market)
3. the more risks there are, the more urgent the institutions need to distribute their shares and hence the higher and longer the bull trap lasts
4. since you don't know what the level each institution is willing to de-risk, how long it goes and what triggers the collapse is all known to you

comment.body:
Gene Seroka, Executive Director of the Port of Los Angeles, warned on CNBC's "Squawk Box" of a 35% drop in cargo volume starting the week of May 5, 2025, due to U.S. tariffs on Chinese goods, with major retailers halting shipments from China.

The Port of Los Angeles, North America's busiest container port, has seen a 44% year-over-year decline in scheduled container ship arrivals from China for the week of May 4-10, 2025, with only 12 ships expected compared to 22 the previous year, per Port Optimizer data.

This supply shock, driven by trade tensions, could disrupt U.S. supply chains, echoing post-COVID disruptions that led to inflation, as supply shocks typically affect specific goods but can have broader economic impacts when widespread, according to Investopedia.
